title: The Mirror Principle

content.statement: Your product's structure ends up mirroring the shape of your org chart.
content.explainer: Systems copy the communication paths of the people who build them, so a fragmented team ships a fragmented product. If you want clean architecture, design the team before you design the system.
questions: Do the seams and handoffs in your product line up suspiciously with the boundaries between your teams?
Are two features awkwardly glued together because two groups that never talk had to build them separately?
If you redrew your org chart around the product you want, how different would it look from today's?
sop: Map your product's major components and note which team or person owns each one.
Count the number of distinct teams or independent contributors involved in building it (see calculation, page 6).
Compute the coordination interfaces that org structure forces on you and treat that as your true complexity cost (see calculation, page 6).
Find every product seam that exists only because two teams had to hand off, not because the design demanded it.
Reshape team boundaries to match the architecture you actually want, then let the product structure follow.

definitions.def: A required line of communication between two teams that must sync to build something together. More teams means more of these connections, each one a place for friction and mismatch.
The map of who reports to whom and which group owns what. This map quietly imprints itself onto whatever the organization builds.

content.equation_example: You have 6 teams building one product. Coordination Interfaces = 6 × (6 − 1) ÷ 2 = 6 × 5 ÷ 2 = 15 communication seams the product must span. More teams means the interface count climbs fast and the product fractures along those lines; fewer, well-bounded teams means cleaner architecture.
content.closing_statement: What you build will always carry the fingerprints of how you organized to build it, so shape the team with intention.
content.question: Do the seams and handoffs in your product line up suspiciously with the boundaries between your teams?

caption:
Principle Nº 331: The Mirror Principle

Your product's structure ends up mirroring the shape of your org chart.

Ask yourself: Do the seams and handoffs in your product line up suspiciously with the boundaries between your teams?
